label: fiction
title: The Quarry Gate
charge: Gross-negligence manslaughter of a maintenance fitter
elements: The accused owed the fitter a duty of care.
His breach of it was truly exceptional negligence.

beats.text: The site manager is unequivocal: 'The shot-firer owns the exclusion zone. The rules are one page long. If a man was inside it when the shot fired, that is on him.'
beats.reveal_note: The rulebook the company waves is the one it starved: the zone he 'owned' had a dead radio the company left dead for six weeks. Responsibility was delegated; the means weren't.

beats.text: The prosecution's timeline lands hard: from siren to blast, forty seconds short of the mandated interval.
beats.reveal_note: Measured from the wrong siren. The quarry sounds two — the plant-yard horn and the blast siren proper. The blast siren's own log shows the full interval, to the second.

beats.text: Six maintenance fault reports, filed by the accused over six weeks, each flagging the exclusion-zone radio as dead — each stamped 'received' by the site office, none actioned.
beats.reveal_note: The paper trail runs the wrong way for the prosecution: the man in the dock is the only person on site who tried, six times, in writing, to fix the thing that killed his colleague.

beats.text: The victim's phone, recovered from the zone: a text from his supervisor, sent after the all-clear sweep, telling him to 'nip back in' for a left tool before the shot.
beats.reveal_note: The fitter was inside the zone because management sent him there — after clearance, past a radio that couldn't warn him. That order was never in the prosecution's timeline.

beats.text: The judge: gross negligence is measured against the system a man was actually given. The jury must ask what more this defendant could have done with the tools he had.
beats.reveal_note: The question that acquits: he swept the zone, sounded the siren, filed the faults. The failures that killed were above his pay grade — and in writing.

twist: The case against the shot-firer was built on the wrong siren and someone else's order. He had reported the dead radio six times in writing; the company filed the reports, and a supervisor texted the fitter back into the zone after clearance. The prosecution stopped one rung too low on the ladder.
